Spears spent 13 years under a conservatorship that legally prevented her from making her own personal and financial  decisions before a Los Angeles judge terminated the arrangement in 2021.
She wrote about her life under the conservatorship in her 2023 memoir, “The Woman in Me."

“I had been so infantilized that I was losing pieces of what made me feel like myself,” Spears wrote. “The conservatorship stripped me of my womanhood, made me into a child. I became more of an entity than a person onstage. I had always felt music in my bones and my blood; they stole that from me.”

Kevin Federline, her ex-husband and the father of her two sons, Sean Preston, 20, and Jayden James, 19, wrote in his memoir released last October, "You Thought You Knew," that he was concerned about Spears.
“The truth is, this situation with Britney feels like it’s racing toward something irreversible,” he added.

“It’s become impossible to pretend everything’s OK,” he continued. “From where I sit, the clock is ticking, and we’re getting close to the 11th hour. Something bad is going to happen if things don’t change, and my biggest fear is that our sons will be left holding the pieces.”
In October 2025, a representative for Spears responded to Federline's book.

“With news from Kevin’s book breaking, once again he and others are profiting off her and sadly it comes after child support has ended with Kevin,” the rep said in a statement to NBC News. “All she cares about are her kids, Sean Preston and Jayden James and their well-being during this sensationalism. She detailed her journey in her memoir.”

In the years since being freed from her conservatorship, she married — and later divorced — Sam Asghari. She has continued to draw attention online for her Instagram posts, which often feature her dancing in front of the camera or sharing stock images with cryptic captions.

title: 'Vladimir' Ending: What Really Happens to Vlad, John and Rachel Weisz's Character?
summary: What happens at the end of Vladimir? Do John and Vladimir survive? What happens to Rachel Weisz's Character?
story: Warning: Spoilers ahead for the finale of “Vladimir.”
When “Vladimir” opens, Rachel Weisz’s unnamed protagonist tells the camera that it has come to her attention that she “may never again have power over another human being.”

The seemingly sweet middle-aged university professor informs viewers that her students find her “out of touch,” her daughter “useless” and “as an older woman” she may never arouse a man ever again and has “lost the ability to captivate.”
She says all this as she walks by Leo Woodall’s Vladimir, a young and handsome new assistant professor — who also happens to be unconscious and chained to a chair.

As the season goes on, viewers discover that Weisz’s character’s husband, John (John Slattery), is accused of having sexual relationships with students. This, she knew about since they have an open marriage.
So when she attempts to get close to Vlad, a father of one and married to Cynthia, who is also teaching at the same university, it doesn’t really faze John.
What happens at the end of “Vladimir” and why is he tied up in her cabin? Read on to find out.
What Happens at the Cabin?
Vlad and Weisz's character have lunch and over wine they discuss their books. She decides to take him to her cabin so they can do some writing and then things take a turn.

She keeps the booze flowing and at one point even puts something in his drink that makes him black out. This is when she ties him up so that he doesn't topple over. While he's unconscious, she uses his cell to text Cynthia and call her out about having an affair with John. She then drops his phone in water to hide her evidence.

When he wakes up, he's confused and screaming. She lies to him and tells him that they got really drunk and he told her he wanted to be “dominated.” She tells him that he got upset after he found out that Cynthia and John were together.

He goes to sleep in the guest room and the next morning she thinks he's gone but he's not. He decides to stay at the cabin, upset that Cynthia gets to do whatever she wants and he doesn't. He also calls out Weisz's character for texting Cynthia. He knows he didn't do it because he saw the exchange on his laptop that's connected to his phone.

They spend the day together, writing their books and having dinner. When they finish eating and she’s cleaning up, Vlad comes up behind her and tells her, “I need to tell you something. I did something bad. I didn’t hand in my paper, professor. Am I in trouble?”
Confused, she excuses herself and goes outside. Vlad follows and apologizes, asking her if that wasn't what they were going to do last night.
She says she doesn't want to play out a fantasy of her being "some pervy older woman." Vlad, however, says he just wants to do what she wants.
“Oh, Vlad, did I make it all up?” she asks about the possible sexual tension between them. “Oh, I’m going to go get ready for bed.”
Vlad replies, “Should I join you?”
He follows her into her bedroom and they start making out. Vlad tells her everything he has been wanting to do to her and then proceeds to do it.
After their moment together, John walks in and Weisz's character and Vlad confront him about being with Cynthia.
John and Cynthia Are Not Together
John tells them that he and Cynthia have not been hooking up.
Instead, they are embarking on an unconventional creative endeavor. They get together at 2 a.m. and take drugs, so that she can finish her memoir and he can work on an epic poem.

Vlad gets upset because Cynthia is a former addict. John tells him that Cynthia just wants to complete her book so they can get out of the condo and “it can stop being about” Vlad all the time. This angers Vlad, who says, “When has it ever been about me?”
He then asks John to borrow his phone so he can call his wife.
What Happened During John's Hearing?
John, who was accused of sexual misconduct with former students, has his hearing in the final episode. Ultimately, the complaints against him are dismissed.

He loses his job but gets to keep his pension, he tells his wife while at the cabin. He also believes that “it was a mistake” to involve their daughter, Sid, as his lawyer because he thinks the testimonies weighed heavy on her.
He then tells her that he would like for them to reconsider their open marriage agreement — after she's done having her fun with Vlad.
What Happens Between Vlad and Rachel Weisz's Character?
When Vlad is done talking to Cynthia on the phone, he and Weisz's character are alone again.
“You want to know what I want?” he asks her, putting a hand over her breast. “I want you to meet me here once a week. Say you will.”
She removes his hand and so he gets up and tells her, “You inspire me.”
Looking at the camera, she says that there was a time when that was the most exciting thing that someone could say to her.
Talking to John, he asks her if her fling was everything she wanted it to be. She says it wasn’t all about desire: It was invigorating and gave her inspiration for her next book.
However, now it appears that she's at odds with who she wants to end up with.
A Fiery Ending to Everyone's Stories
Sitting on the couch, deciding how her new book will end, she gets inspired knowing that Vlad and John think of her as a character in their own stories.
In her story, as she describes, “A fire breaks out when we’re asleep, just like in a gothic novel. As though, that obsession, that thrill of desire goes up in flames.”
She wakes up to the space heater causing a fire and runs to John and Vlad's rooms. It's difficult to comprehend if what she's describing is happening in real life or in her book.
Vlad and John try to get out of the cabin but the back door is stuck. She sees her stacks of notepads, the ones she's been writing her novel in, on the coffee table near the fire.
She grabs her notepads and the rug behind her catches fire, seemingly leaving Vlad and John stuck and unable to get out.
She’s then seen furiously writing on the couch, no fire in sight.
“And this is how it plays out,” she says in the voiceover, before she’s seen standing outside, notepads in hand, staring at the cabin going up in flames.

Looking at the camera, she says, “I finish my book about a woman’s obsession with her younger colleague. Vlad writes a book about a tender affair with an older professor. Mine does much better. It speaks to a certain need. Oh, don’t worry, I call 9-1-1-. Everybody gets out.”
“You don’t believe me?” she asks, turning to the camera with a smirk as windows explode and fire truck sirens are heard in the back.
The episode ends without knowing for certain if Vlad and John made it out alive.

title: Sam Heughan Shares the Moment Audience 'Erupted' at 'Outlander' Season 8 Premiere
summary: “Outlander” star Sam Heughan is revealing a special moment viewers can look forward to in the upcoming Season 8 premiere, out March 6.
story: After more than a decade of watching Claire and Jamie’s love story unfold across multiple timelines, the couple will share just one more season of "Outlander" together.
One of the stars is revealing a special moment viewers can look forward to in the Season 8 premiere on March 6.
The Starz period drama, which debuted in 2014, was renewed for its eighth and final season in January 2023, as Deadline reported.

Dedicated viewers have had two years to prepare to bid Claire (Caitriona Balfe) and Jamie (Sam Heughan) farewell, and the moment is nearly here. Season 8, which will include 10 episodes, premieres on March 6.
As the countdown to the end draws closer, Sam Heughan took some time to stop by TODAY with Jenna & Sheinelle to chat about the series conclusion and the “emotional goodbye.”

During his interview with Jenna Bush Hager and Sheinelle Jones on March 4, the actor said that after filming 101 episodes of the show, it was “really beautiful to come back to New York to have our premiere the other night to share that moment with the fans.”
He added, “I’ve had time to process it. But it’s been a quarter of my life playing this character. So yeah, it’s been hard to say goodbye, but bittersweet as well.”
The New York premiere included a screening of the first episode of Season 8. Heughan revealed a small spoiler from the episode that he said led to a strong reaction from the audience.
“Roger (Richard Rankin) and Bree (Sophie Skelton) come back from the future, and when they appear the whole audience kind of erupted,” he shared.
Brianna is Claire and Jamie’s second daughter. She and her husband Roger had taken their children to the future to get their daughter life-saving surgery.

Heughan couldn’t reveal more details about Season 8, though he confirmed he hasn’t seen the ending yet. He also told Jenna and Sheinelle the conclusion was “kept under wraps” and that “only a few people knew the ending.”
“There were even fake endings,” he continued. “There were fake characters made up.”
But he assured fans that it would be a great sendoff. “What I believe (to be) how it ends is a very beautiful moment,” he said.

title: Boston Rob Reveals His Top 4 Traitors and Shares His Advice for ‘Survivor 50’
summary: Rob “Boston Rob” Mariano talks "The Traitors" finale, including his thought son Rob Rausch, "Survivor 50" and more.
story: Rob “Boston Rob” Mariano is always jumping from one project to the next, but he never forgets his roots.

The reality television icon has been delighting fans with his masterful gameplay across multiple competition shows, most notably “Survivor,” for over two decades. But recently, he decided to dissect game dynamics from a different perspective as he put on his hosting hat to lead the semifinals of the Global Dealer Technician Challenge video series. The competition, which named a winner in Las Vegas on March 3 at the CONEXPO-CON/AGG, brought together top technicians from around the world for a live competition where they showed off their skills. Construction manufacturer Caterpillar tapped Boston Rob to oversee a round of the event given his background.

Boston Rob was a 25-year-old construction worker when he competed for the first time on “Survivor: Marquesas” in 2002 and, in doing so, became a legend. In an interview with TODAY.com, the television personality says he jumped at the opportunity to host because it was the perfect bridge between his roots in construction and his reality television journey.
“In a way, I got to play Jeff Probst for the weekend for the challenge,” he says, a nod to the “Survivor” host.

He continues, “I worked in construction a lot of years, and there’s a lot of parallels to what these guys are doing. Fixing these machines and keeping them running and adapting to different situations. I think it was really cool that Caterpillar was able to create a challenge to celebrate these guys.”

Boston Rob notes that there is a shortage of technicians right now, and says he hoped that collaborating with Caterpillar would bring awareness to the profession and how integral the workers are to making sure everyone can access basic necessities, like roads and internet. He also says that becoming a technician requires adaptability and quick-thinking, two skills that have been pivotal in his impressive reality television career.

Below, Boston Rob opens up more about his construction background and also shares his thoughts on “Survivor 50,” “The Traitors” Season 4 finale, Rob Rausch’s victory, his YouTube series “Everything’s a Competition” with Dylan Efron and more.
This interview has been edited and condensed for clarity.

I think it’s being able to adapt to the job on the fly. All the time situations arise when you’re working in construction that you don’t anticipate, whether it’s you open up a floor and find out the pipes are bad, or you’re digging in a hole and you see something exposed. You have to make decisions. That happens in “Survivor” all the time. I’ve always said, the people that are best able to adapt, they’re the ones that ultimately end up doing the best. So what was unique about this challenge that Caterpillar did was they created scenarios for these techs to test how good they are, not unlike some of the challenges that we finished in reality TV, where they’re trying to throw you off the course. You know, I got a reputation for being pretty good at figuring out things on the fly.

It’s hard to tell. I went to college and graduated with a degree in psychology. But ultimately, I ended up using that in “Survivor.”  But I love to work with my hands and build things. “Survivor”  definitely changed the course of my life because I met my wife (Amber Mariano). I have four beautiful children because of it. So I’m super grateful for everything that came into my life because of “Survivor.” But I still work in construction today. I have a renovation company, and I was working yesterday on my house, working on my garage. I think that’s in me, that’s in my blood. It’s something I love to do, and I’m proud of it.

He made my ears bleed for three years, kind of asking me different types of strategies and what I’d do. I kind of gave him the playbook. I was like, “Listen, you’re not me so you can’t play like I would. But if I were you, this is what I would do.” I outlined a scenario that I thought would help him in his game.
The first thing is, he’s a big, intimidating guy — “Make sure you smile,” (I told him). Because if you just look at him on the surface, he’s scary. He’s like, 6’4,” 280 (pounds) all muscle.

Then I told him, “Worry about what you can control. There’s things that are going to happen in that game that you’re not going to have any say in. Don’t worry about that. Focus on winning. Remember that not everybody out there is there to help you win. They’re there to help them win and try to see the game through their eyes and their perspective.”

I also told him when he gets home, if he gets voted out first, not to call me or come back. Luckily, he didn’t. So we’re excited about that. He’s actually coming over to the house to watch (Episode 2) tonight (March 4).

The premiere was great. If anything, it showed the audience’s affinity (and) enthusiasm towards the old school game. Three hours was long, but I watched the whole thing. They got me in my seat the whole time. It’s hard to say who’s going to go next or who’s going to go far, because this game, I’ve played it so many times that I know to always expect the unexpected. “Survivor” and Probst, they do a great job with making sure that they keep the contestants on their toes. But I think there’s something to be said for momentum, too. So the teams that are winning usually continue to win. It’s a big thing in “Survivor.”

It’s tough because Cirie and Ozzy have really big reputations. Anytime there’s a game with people that are coming back and playing again, you’re going to face that. As far as who I would specifically target, I guess it would have to depend (on) what tribe I was put on. Generally, the people that are kind of the unknowns, the people from Season 49 (Savannah Louie and Rizo Velovic), that they haven’t seen their game, to me, there’s a reason why they earned their spot on such a monumental season. So I’d be pretty wary of those two.

Then, if I had any history with anyone that I thought was going to help her, I’d either align with them or get them out. On this specific season, there’s nobody that I think would be a huge threat to me. But I also know if I were out there, I’d be public enemy number one. I always am.
Yep. Wednesday night is “Survivor Night” at our house. For sure we watch them.

I think Rob’s definitely up there. I think Cirie played a great game the first season. You know we didn’t win, but I put myself and Bob the Drag Queen up there, too. I feel like we understood the game, and unfortunately, we weren’t on the same page. But if we ever were, I think we’d make a pretty dynamic duo.

He’s awesome. Bob is a really talented individual. Reality TV is just one spectrum of the stuff that he does. So it was really awesome to have truly a partner that understood, not only “Traitors” from a strategic point, but we joke about the fashion. I learned a lot from Bob. I really enjoyed having him as a co-host. It was fun.

Sometimes you do these shows and people have a lot of hang ups that they can do something about. But they get hung up on the show and what happened to them, and it affects them after the game. Luckily, Bob wasn’t like that at all. He was like, “Well, it was a game. It’s fair play. Whatever. It happened.” We’re able to have a great relationship and I’d even say a friendship. It’s really cool that he was able to get to that place. I’ve always been able to, so just cool he was of the same mindset.

I don’t know. Maybe he’d watched the seasons. I’ve connected with Rob and (he) seems like a fun guy. I think he played the game to the best of his ability, and it showed. He crushed it. So I got nothing but respect for him.
Bring them all on. I don’t care. I don’t have any hang ups against anyone. All’s fair in love and war, and in the Scotland “Traitors” castle.

I don’t know if it would ever happen, but if we could get Probst in that castle, that would be pretty epic. He always claims that he’d be a good gamer. I’d love to see him in action. Let’s see if he can put his money where his mouth is.

Dylan and I just have a really good relationship and friendship. We bonded in the castle. I see a lot of myself in him. It just feels easy. We hang out, we have fun, we like the same things, we’re both athletic, we’re both competitive and it’s just a natural fit. I can’t tell you why it works, but it just does. I feel like I’ve been a pretty good judge of character my whole life. I know what I like, I know who I get along with, and my people are my people. He’s one of my boys.

I think he’d be great on “Survivor.” I actually talked to him this morning about some strategies. He’s over there now. He’s an outdoors guy. I think at some point, though, he’s going to have a target because he’s been doing all these shows and doing so well with, like, “Dancing With the Stars.” So I think that’s the one thing that he has to be aware of for sure.

It’s actually a Fire Making Challenge. This has to do with Dylan’s obsession with “Survivor.” He’s like, “Have you ever actually done (it)?” I was like, “No, I didn’t do this Fire Making Challenge.” He’s like, “We should do it.“ I was like, “Bro, this is a lose, lose for me.” He’s like, “What do you mean?” I was like, “Because if I win and smoke you, people are going to be like, ‘Oh, of course, Rob is going to win. It’s a layup.’ And if, by some miracle, you beat me, I’d never hear the end of it.” So, long story short, he got his way and we did it. You’ll have to wait to see what happened. But the construction stuff was, he was like, “How are we going to do it?” That was in his backyard in LA. He had an old bed frame, and I turned the bed frame and built two fire making stations out of it. It’ll be out Thursday (March 5).

title: Release Dates for Next 2 ‘ACOTAR’ Books Revealed by Sarah J. Maas
summary: Sarah J. Maas announced two more "ACOTAR" books and the release dates for each.
story: Light the beacons and ready the gates, Sarah J. Maas fans, because this Suriel tea is piping hot: The sixth “ACOTAR” book you’ve been waiting for finally has a release date.
Oh, and a seventh book has one, too.
The author of the wildly popular “A Court of Thorns and Roses” series — as well as the “Throne of Glass” and “Crescent City” books — has revealed dates for her next two drops:
Oct. 27, 2026: “ACOTAR” 6Jan. 12, 2027: “ACOTAR” 7
Oct. 27, 2026: “ACOTAR” 6
Jan. 12, 2027: “ACOTAR” 7

This news, shared by Maas in her highly anticipated appearance on the “Call Her Daddy” podcast, did not accompany titles or cover art — but she did share that these books will be part of one big story told in three books.

“It came out of me in a way that surprised me,” she told host Alex Cooper. “I like to do parts: part one, part two, part three. By the time I got to the end of part one, it was, like, 400 pages long.”
The first part of this story will be told in “ACOTAR” 6 and the second two parts will be in “ACOTAR” 7. The fourth and final part of this story will come down the line.
“It’s meant to be read as one massive, massive story,” she said.
"A Court of Thorns and Roses 6" (Pre-Order) by Sarah J. Maas
"A Court of Thorns and Roses 7" (Pre-Order) by Sarah J. Maas

This is a significant update for Maas‘ legions of fans, who scour her interviews and social posts for even the most minor hints. The last time Maas shared a development was with us at TODAY in January 2024, right after her latest book release. She had her next four books planned, Maas said, the next one being an “ACOTAR” book. The one after that is a book she’s “very excited about” but wouldn’t say what series it relates to.

Maas, who made her debut in publishing in 2012, currently has a sprawling catalogue of 16 books spanning three different romantasy series — “A Court of Thorns and Roses,” better known as “ACOTAR,” as well as “Throne of Glass” and “Crescent City” — all of which have massive fan bases and thrust Maas on to bestseller lists.

“ACOTAR” is often the gateway into the Maas-iverse, though Maas hasn’t released a book in that series since 2021’s “A Court of Silver Flames.” The last two books Maas released, in 2022 and 2024, were part of her “Crescent City” series. In 2023, Maas signed a new deal with her publisher, Bloomsbury, to add four more books to the three she already had under contract at the time.

“ACOTAR” celebrated its 10th anniversary last spring. The first novel introduces readers to the world of Prythian, where immortal beings called Fae preside over a system of courts, at times feuding with each other and other lands in their world.

The first three books in the series followed Feyre, a human girl who is introduced to the world of the Fae and brought to the Spring Court — “Beauty and the Beast” style — after killing one of the Fae. Over the course of “ACOTAR” and its next two sequels, “A Court of Mist and Fury” and “A Court of Wings and Ruin,” Feyre not only turns Fae, she becomes High Lady of one of the most feared courts.

Maas continued the series with a novella, “A Court of Frost and Starlight,” before switching focus in “A Court of Silver Flames,” told from the dual perspectives of Feyre’s caustic and traumatized oldest sister, Nesta, and her love interest, Cassian.

title: Teenagers Are Obsessed with 'Love Story' — and Their Moms' Real Memories from the '90s
summary: “Love Story,” a new FX series that relives John F. Kennedy Jr, is bringing together moms and daughters who love t.
story: “Love Story,” a new FX series that relives John F. Kennedy Jr. and Carolyn Bessette’s relationship, is bridging a generational gap.
Since it premiered on Feb. 12, the series has inspired a cultural sensation and caught the attention of a new generation that has become fascinated with the couple’s romance.
Some younger viewers are tuning in with their mothers to glean firsthand insight into what it was really like to follow the couple’s love story in real time.
As viewers continue to follow “Love Story” as it unfolds, NBC News Correspondent Anne Thompson sat down with a few girls who have bonded with their mothers while watching the series.
Phoebe Aston, an 11-year-old who has been tuning in with her 14-year-old sister Olivia Aston and their mother, explained why “Love Story” captivated her from the very first episode.
“I think the way their love story connects because at the beginning Carolyn was playing kind of hard to get. It really intrigued me to get more interested and find more about it,” Phoebe Aston said.
Before watching “Love Story,” some young viewers only had limited knowledge of Kennedy and Bessette’s popularity.
“I knew of him. I knew who he was. But I don’t think I knew much about the backstory of who JFK Jr. was, how he lived his life,” 14-year-old Sloan Corleto said.
Meanwhile, 14-year-old Olivia Aston said she knew a little bit about Bessette before watching the series.

“I’ve definitely looked up Carolyn Bessette. I think she was kind of an icon. And I’ve looked up a lot about their family and the history of the Kennedys cause of course, watching a show like that, you just want to know more,” she said.
Olivia Aston first heard about the show from her mother.

“She’s very enthusiastic about it. Of course, she lived through the time. And we started watching it together, and we really loved it. It’s really nice,” she said. “She’ll fill me in on things that I don’t understand and she’ll talk about what it was like to live during that time period.”
Phoebe Aston was also struck by the show’s spot-on casting.

“I remember the first episode I watched with my mom and my sister. I immediately went on my phone and searched them and I was like, ‘Oh, Mom, they look so similar to the actors.’ And I was watching YouTube videos and I was so intrigued,” she said.
Sloan Corleto’s mother, Courtney Corleto, has relished in the opportunity to share memories with her daughter while enjoying the series together.
“You’d have friends tell stories like, ‘Oh, I was walking in Central Park and I saw him playing football with friends.’ So, yeah, you always kind of knew of them,” she said.
Sloan Corleto has also been intrigued by Bessette’s sense of style while watching the series and even purchased a tortoiseshell headband to channel her look.
While reflecting on the couple’s level of celebrity, Sloane Corleto said she can't find a modern equivalent.
“I’m not quite sure if anybody today can meet his kind of level,” she said.
Kennedy and Bessette died in a plane crash in 1999 along with her sister Lauren Bessette. While watching the series, Courtney Corleto can’t help but think about what could have been.

“I think they were so young and there was so much promise. And what were they to become? What would their family look like? What were they going to achieve? And I just think that a life cut so short and so bright, I think it really was tragic,” she said.

title: ‘SNL’ Greats Reveal All About Lorne Michaels In First Documentary Trailer
summary: TODAY revealed an exclusive clip of a trailer for the "Lorne," a documentary about Lorne Michaels.
story: He’s helped bring four of the most famous words in television to life: “Live from New York.” Now, fans can focus on four other words: Who is Lorne Michaels?
A sneak peek at the trailer for “Lorne,” an upcoming documentary about the life and career of “Saturday Night Live” creator and producer Lorne Michaels, was unveiled March 5 exclusively on TODAY.

The trailer features multiple snippets of classic sketches from the long-running comedy series, as well as interviews with current and former cast members, writers, hosts and musical artists from the show, including Tina Fey, Chris Rock, Seth Meyers, Conan O’Brien, Mike Myers, Colin Jost, Maya Rudolph, Andy Samberg, Kristen Wiig, Paul Simon and Michaels himself.
“I was probably too driven to be a hippie. What I wanted was a voice in the culture, and ‘SNL’ was that vehicle,” he says.
“All of life is reinvention, so to be understood? Not going to happen,” he says later.
Indeed, the trailer hints at how mysterious Michaels can be.
“He has this man behind the curtain mystique about him,” Wiig says.

“He’s going to have notes that are impossible to understand. You’ll know it’s not playing the way Lorne thought it was going to play when he does this,” Meyers said, as he filled his cheeks with air and blew, just before a clip of Michaels doing the same plays.
“It feels like he’s blowing your soul out of your body,” Jost says.
“For all I know, he works for the CIA,” Rock says.
O’Brien, meanwhile, points out how Michaels has remained a constant in an industry that is anything but.
“Lorne is the ultimate show business survivor. He’s still here and hundreds of executives are not,” O’Brien says.

Michaels has won 24 Emmy Awards in his career, while “Saturday Night Live” has been a launching pad for scores of comedians over the last five decades. The show celebrated its 50th anniversary in 2025, while its first-ever episode was also the subject of a 2024 movie, “Saturday Night.”
“Lorne” opens in theaters April 17 and is directed by Morgan Neville, who won an Academy Award in 2014 for his documentary “20 Feet from Stardom.”

title: Romantic or Toxic? Carolyn Bessette-Kennedy's Biographer Weighs in on the Couple's Real Love Story
summary: What was JFK Jr and Carolyn Bessette's love story really like? Was it romantic or toxic? Elizabeth Beller's biographer
story: More than 25 years after their deaths, Carolyn Bessette and John F. Kennedy Jr. remains frozen in cultural memory: beautiful, glamorous, doomed.

But as a new generation discovers them, and as Ryan Murphy’s "Love Story" reimagines their romance, the old question resurfaces: was theirs an epic love story or a troubled marriage unraveling behind the scenes?

"Love Story" leans into the electricity. Carolyn is portrayed as magnetic and emotionally attuned — deeply caring and empathetic, yet grounded in a strong sense of self. She gives John a hard time before agreeing to date him, not out of calculation, but because she refuses to be swept up by his last name. Their chemistry crackles in part because she isn’t dazzled by the Kennedy mystique; she meets him as an equal.

Conflict surfaces quickly, too: an anonymous letter attacking Carolyn’s character drives a wedge between them, highlighting the outside forces that would repeatedly intrude on their relationship. The show's fifth episode depicts their infamous fight in a Manhattan park covered extensively by tabloids.
But beyond dramatization lies a more layered truth.

Elizabeth Beller, author of "Once Upon a Time: The Captivating Life of Carolyn Bessette-Kennedy," spent years speaking to those who knew the couple best. What she found complicates the “troubled marriage” narrative that tabloids cemented in the late ’90s.

“I think it’s two people who loved each other very much and had a lot on their plates,” Beller tells TODAY.com. “Young marriages need time, and they had so many pressures on them, and then you add the public eye and a constant paparazzi. It just creates a pressure cooker.”
That pressure cooker often translated, publicly, into stories of fights and strain. But Beller pushes back on the idea that conflict equals collapse.

“I land on, they really loved each other, but there were realities that made things very difficult,” she explains. “I don’t know any couple … if you don’t fight, you’re not living in the same house. So even if they fought and there was turmoil, that does not mean there was not a deep love.”
Friends of the couple told Beller that Kennedy and Bessette shared not just devotion, but joy.
“They really laughed a lot together,” she says. “And I think, you know, that’s a great way to fall in love.”
Much of the darker mythology surrounding their marriage, Beller argues, grew from Bessette's refusal to perform for the press.

“It was very clear she didn’t want to be in the public eye,” Beller says. “That shows that her value system was different than being about celebrity and fame. She was interested in John for who John was. She was interested in their life for what they could do, how they could be of service.”

When Bessette wouldn’t provide a narrative, one was created for her. “There was sort of a narrative built when she didn’t want to give them one,” Beller says. “And I think it was misrepresentative of her.”
Their marriage, by many accounts, was defined less by dysfunction than by intensity — two ambitious, strong-willed people navigating extraordinary scrutiny.

When attention grew too strong, Bessette had to give up her job at Calvin Klein, losing not just a career but a daily support system. Kennedy was running George magazine and contemplating a future in politics. The glare was constant; the stakes, unusually high.
We’ll never know what their relationship might have become with time and privacy.
“The sad part is we didn’t get a chance to find out,” Beller says. The couple died, along with Bessette's sister Lauren Bessette, in a plane crash in 1999.

What remains is not simply a fairy tale or a cautionary tale, but a portrait of two people who loved each other deeply, argued like real couples do, laughed often and carried, perhaps unfairly, the weight of a nation’s projections.

title: Colleen Hoover Explains Why She's Still 'Very Proud' of 'It Ends with Us'
summary: Colleen Hoover speaks about her thoughts on It Ends with Us amid the impending lawsuit and legal drama between Justin Baldoni and Blake Lively.
story: Bestselling author Colleen Hoover sat down with TODAY’s Jenna Bush Hager for an episode of her “Open Book with Jenna” podcast that aired on March 5.
During the conversation, Hoover and Jenna spoke about her astonishing career (75 million books sold and counting), her health and, of course, her headline-making first book-to-screen adaptation.

Hoover’s “It Ends with Us,” a novel she said was based on her mother’s life and experience in an abusive marriage, became a film in 2024. Hoover previously shared with Jenna that one of her earliest memories was of her father throwing a TV at her mother. Her parents later divorced. The book follows a woman who, after growing up with domestic abusive, finds herself in a new cycle of violence in a romantic relationship.

The production led to a tangled lawsuit between the movie’s director and star Justin Baldoni, and its star and executive producer, Blake Lively. The legal battle began in December 2024 when Lively filed a complaint against Baldoni alleging sexual harassment and retaliation.
“Is it hard to have those two things, like your very first memory, one of your pieces of art that I know you cherish, wrapped up with all of this?” Jenna asked Hoover in the "Open Book" episode.

Hoover first spoke out about the lawsuit in a November 2025 Elle Magazine interview, calling it “sad” and saying she hesitates to recommend the book. But speaking to Jenna, Hoover said her relationship to the book is softening as time passes.

“You know, it is, but it’s getting easier to separate it because I do look at the success of it. Like opening week, we just were all so shocked at how much readers showed up for it and how well it did and how much it resonated. I’m so proud of that movie,” she said.
The film grossed $350 million worldwide against a $25 million budget according to IMDB.
Hoover applauded the entire team behind the film without naming specific individuals.

“I think the actors, the director, everyone did such a good job making it that I have to look at everything else separate from the film, from the book,” she explained. “As unfortunate as it is, I am still just very much grateful that we got to see it come to life. And, for me, I’m very proud of that film.”

Lively, who played protagonist Lily Bloom, filed a complaint with the California Civil Rights Department against Baldoni in December 2024 alleging sexual harassment and retaliation on the set. That same month, Lively formalized her complaint into a federal civil lawsuit against Baldoni in New York.

Baldoni, meanwhile, filed a $250 million libel lawsuit against The New York Times in December and a $400 million defamation lawsuit against Lively and her husband, Ryan Reynolds. Both were dismissed in June 2025 by a district court judge.
In an interview with Elle Magazine in November 2025, Hoover called the legal drama “unfortunate” and “disappointing.”

“It feels like a circus,” she said. “When there are real people involved, with real feelings and emotions. This actually truly has impacted some of the actors’ careers in huge ways. And I just find it all around sad.”

In February 2026, Lively and Baldoni took part in a court-ordered settlement conference but failed to reach a resolution, per NBC News. The case will head to trial in May if the former co-stars can’t reach a settlement.

title: BTS Says '7 Together, We Can Do Anything' in 1st Trailer for Netflix Concert
summary: The countdown is on for BTS’ highly anticipated return — and Netflix is sharing a sneak peek.
story: The countdown is on for BTS’ highly anticipated return — and Netflix is sharing a sneak peek.
On March 4, the streaming service released the first trailer for the K-pop group’s upcoming live concert, “BTS The Comeback Live | Arirang.”
The trailer begins with footage of an empty studio and the group on stage, while fans share via voiceover how much they miss the group.
BTS member RM is then heard saying, “We promised our fans that we’d be back,” as scenes of each individual member flash across the screen.
After RM appears, Jin, Suga, J-Hope, Jimin, V and Jung Kook are each introduced.
“I felt that each and every one of us was evolving in some way,” V says in Korean, while another member adds, “Seven together, we can do anything. Keep swimming.”
"BTS the Comeback Live" will stream live from Gwanghwamun in Seoul on Netflix on March 21 at 8 p.m. KST/4 a.m. PT/ 7 a.m. ET.
The special will celebrate the group’s release of their album “Arirang,” out March 20.

This marks the first album and performance since all seven members completed their mandatory military service last year. The group will also be embarking on their “BTS World Tour Arirang,” which kicks off April 9 in South Korea.
BTS has been teasing their upcoming album by sharing the tracklist on their social media. Army will get 14 new songs, with some titled “Body to Body,” “Hooligan,” “Like Animals” and “Into the Sun.”

Fans will have plenty of BTS to look forward to in 2026. In addition to the live concert stream, people will be able to watch select BTS concerts in theaters, as well as get a behind the scenes look at the making of their album and return to the stage in the Netflix documentary, “BTS: The Return,” dropping March 27.

title: Harry Styles Reflects on Liam Payne's Death: 'It's Difficult to Lose a Friend'
summary: Harry Styles is sharing for the first time how he “struggled” following the sudden passing of friend and late One Direction member Liam Payne.
story: Harry Styles is sharing for the first time how he “struggled” following the sudden passing of friend and late One Direction member Liam Payne.

Payne, a member of the British boy band along with Styles, Zayn Malik, Niall Horan and Louis Tomlinson, tragically died on Oct. 16, 2024 at the age of 31 after falling from a third floor balcony in Argentina.

Shortly after the news of his death circulated, Styles and each of his fellow former band members posted emotional tributes to Payne. The “Aperture” singer said on Instagram, in part, “I am truly devastated by Liam’s passing. His greatest joy was making other people happy, and it was an honour to be alongside him as he did.”
When Styles sat down for an interview on Apple Music’s “The Zane Lowe Show” posted on March 4, he opened up about how he was affected by Payne’s death.
During the conversation, Styles mentioned the vulnerability that comes with being an artist, which made host Zane Lowe bring up Payne.
“I just thought of Liam when you said that, you know, I thought of some, and not just Liam, but people who struggled to get out of extraordinary situations,” Lowe said.

Styles replied, “I mean, full transparency, it’s like something that I, even the idea of talking about it, I struggle with that a little bit even. I think there was a period when he passed away where I really struggled with kind of, like, acknowledging how strange it is to have people kind of, like, own part of your grief in a way.”

“I have such strong feelings around my friend passing away,” he continued. “Then suddenly being, you know, aware of, there’s maybe like, a desire from other people of you to convey that in some way, or it means you’re not feeling what you’re feeling or something, you know?”
He then shared how he is managing his grief and why Payne’s death made him re-examine his own life.

“It’s so difficult to lose a friend. It’s difficult to lose any friend, but it’s so difficult to lose a friend who is so like you in so many ways,” he explained. “It’s like, I saw someone with the kindest heart who just wanted to be great.”
After Payne died, Styles said he asked himself, “’OK, what do I want to do with my life? How do I want to live my life?’”
The Grammy-winner said he realized he needed to appreciate life more.
“I think the greatest way you can honor your friends who pass away is by living your life to the fullest,” he said, before praising the late musician for being a “super special person.”
He described his death as “really sad” before the two moved on to other topics.
Styles follows Tomlinson, who also spoke at length about how much he valued his friendship with Payne.

While appearing on the “The Diary Of A CEO” podcast in October, Tomlinson said about Payne, “There are just too many words and too many memories. It could just be infinite, the post… I really wanted him to be remembered the way that he should be remembered.”

title: Sheinelle Jones Shares the Sweet Reason Behind Her Name's Unique Spelling
summary: What is the meaning of Sheinelle's name? Sheinelle Jones shares the origin story of her name on Jenna and Sheinelle.
story: Sheinelle Jones’ name has a beautiful backstory.
During the chat on March 4, the TODAY with Jenna and Sheinelle co-host opened up about the origins of her first name and what makes it so meaningful.
“I know the ‘I’ makes no sense, but my mom is Sheila and then my dad is Darnell,” she told Jenna Bush Hager.
As it turns out, Sheinelle’s aunt is responsible for the lyrical amalgamation of the two names.
“Aunt Patricia came up with, ‘What if we put their names together and it makes Sheinelle?’” she continued.
Jenna was impressed.
“That’s like the early Bennifer,” she said, referring to the popular combination of Ben Affleck and Jennifer Lopez’s names while they were romantically involved.
Sheinelle agreed that her aunt was “ahead of her time.”
She also acknowledged that the unique spelling of her name has caused some challenges for her over the years.

“I had a news director once who asked me to change the spelling of my name. He was like, ‘You should make it Chanel just so that everybody can understand what it is,’” she recalled. “And at the time I thought maybe I should. And then I was like, ‘No, that’s not my name.’”
Jenna said she’s proud of Sheinelle for keeping the original spelling of her name.
“I love that you kind of stuck to your guns,” she said.
“Which is why this show is so special,” Sheinelle added, referencing the name TODAY with Jenna & Sheinelle.
Jenna said Sheinelle is lucky to have been named after “two of the people who you love most in the world.”

In 2018, Sheinelle took to social media to poke fun at the unique spelling of her name. On Instagram, she shared a photo of an Elle magazine story that mentioned her and revealed that she’s used the magazine’s name as a way to describe the spelling of her name for a long time.

“For YEARS I’ve explained how to remember to spell my name: ‘SHE-IN-ELLE magazine’ ... and now I really am! I’m in Elle! My picture at least ... alongside @beyonce & @lupitanyongo as examples of how some of us showcased black excellence for Halloween! So beyond awesome!!” she wrote.
Sheinelle continued her post, explaining that her moniker is a combination of her parents’ names.
“Side note — my mom’s name is ‘Sheila,’ my father is ‘Darnell’ — put it together and it equals ‘Sheinelle,’” she wrote.

title: Eric Nam Says Rob Rausch Sent Him This 3-Word DM After ‘The Traitors’ Finale
summary: Eric Nam reveals how Rob Rausch reached out to him after betraying the singer in the Season 4 finale of "The Traitors."
story: Eric Nam had to take a beat after his time on Season 4 of “The Traitors.”
The singer-songwriter was recruited to be a Traitor mid-season after Rob Rausch successfully ousted fellow Traitors Lisa Rinna and Candiace Dillard Bassett of “The Real Housewives” franchise.
Eric and Rob made it to the end, but only the “Love Island USA” star walked away with the $220,800 prize pot.

At the final banishment, in a move that genuinely seemed to shock Eric, Rob betrayed him by igniting another round of voting and eliminating him. If he hadn’t, then Rob and Eric would have shared the money. Instead, Rob and Maura Higgins, the final Faithful, voted to banish Eric, leaving Rob the last Traitor standing — and the game show’s solo winner.
Eric tells TODAY.com he received a message from Rob about two to three days after the show finished filming.
“I got a DM from Rob that said, ‘My fault, G,’” Eric recalls — and adds his reaction. “Then I was like, ‘I can’t right now.’ I was like, ‘I’m not responding right now.’”
The next day, Eric says he mustered up a reply.
“I took some time and the next day I think said, ‘Haha, we’re good. No worries,’” he says.

Eric recently released a song, “How the Fire Started,” inspired by his time on “The Traitors” and recruited Rob to star, along with Maura and fellow contestants Ron Funches, Natalie Anderson and Yamil ‘Yam Yam’ Arocho.
In the music video, Eric and the other “Traitors” contestants betrayed by Rob attempt to murder him.
“I processed the emotions, put it into a song, and then I was like, wait, I need them in this video,” Eric says. “This is my ‘Traitor’ game right there.”

When Eric appeared on TODAY with Jenna & Sheinelle alongside Rob and Maura on March 3, the singer said he called Rob and told him, “‘I need you to be in my next music video.’ (Rob) said, ‘It’s literally the least I could do.’”
Maura said on TODAY with Jenna & Sheinelle they had a “great day” and filming was “a lot of fun.”
Compared to Lisa, who told Vulture she needed three weeks to recover from “Traitors,” Eric only needed a text from Rob to move on.
Maura, despite her shock at Rob’s reveal, said she got over the betrayal quickly, too.
“I knew that it was a game. I went straight from the Scottish castle to Fiji to film another show. It’s not deep for me. It’s a made up game,” she says.
Rob, meanwhile, walked away with the prize money and just one promise: a Birkin bag for Maura.

title: Valerie Bertinelli Says After 2 Marriages, She's 'Never Experienced' Real Love
summary: Valerie Bertinelli says she hasn't experienced love after two marriages. She was married to Eddie Van Halen and Tom Vitale.
story: While looking back on her two divorces in a new interview with People, Valerie Bertinelli made some vulnerable confessions about her love life and revealed that she’s “never really experienced” true love.

The 65-year-old was most recently married to Tom Vitale from 2011 to 2022. Previously, she was married to musician Eddie Van Halen, with whom she welcomed a son in 1991. Van Halen died in 2020 after a battle with cancer.
During the interview, Bertinelli described the impact that her two divorces had on her outlook on love.

“Two divorces behind me, I felt like a failure. I didn’t know what love actually meant, really, real true (love). Ed and I had it for glimpses, but I really never really experienced it for any length of time. And I still haven’t. You know, Ed was the closest I’ve ever come to feeling true true love,” she said.
Two years after her divorce from Vitale, Bertinelli went public with her boyfriend Mike Goodnough. The couple went their separate ways less than a year later.
Bertinelli was quick to acknowledge that one major thing has made her hesitant to dive into romantic relationships in the past.

“I don’t feel safe and I don’t trust people a lot. So safety is a huge thing for me. And it’s fascinating that I don’t always pick men that make me feel safe. Or, they do in the beginning and then they change their minds,” she said.
Looking to the future, Bertinelli is open to romance if it’s with the right person, but she’s not eager to fall head over heels.
“I’m very happy being alone right now because I feel safer. I don’t know how to feel safe around a man. I know I can. I know I’ve learned a lot,” she said.
Looking ahead to the future, Bertinelli knows exactly what she’s looking for in a partner and when she will share him with the rest of the world.

“And if I ever do start dating again, I ain’t talking about it for a long time. I gotta see if it works out first,” she said. “I don’t want butterflies. I don’t like them. It feels like anxiety. Limerence is not love. Don’t want that. I want calm. I want safe. I want emotional safety. I haven’t had that in a long time. In fact, I’ve had quite the opposite of it where it’s frightening.”
In return, Bertinelli wants to give her future partner the same level of security.

“I want someone to feel emotionally safe with me, like they can open up and be vulnerable with me because I’ll protect that and I want to feel protected. And I haven’t experienced that either where my vulnerability has been respected and held on to,” she said.
When her marriage to Vitale ended, Bertinelli said she was tempted to swear off romance forever.
“After my second divorce, I was like, ‘I hate men,’” she recalled.
Now, she’s more open to a relationship when the time is right.

“I mean, love, when it’s right, can be magical. And one day I might get that, but I also have to be OK with one day not getting it. So that’s where I’m at. I love my life right now. Every time I think I want to share it with a man, I’m like, ‘But do I?’” she said.

title: 4 Books to Read this March, According to Kwame Alexander
summary: These are the best books to read in March 2026 according to Kwame Alexander.
story: Kwame Alexander stopped by TODAY on March 4 to share the books he's excited about this month.
They include a story of hope and redemption, a thriller that's about to be turned into an AppleTV+ series and a collection of short stories from a two-time Pulitzer Prize-winning author.
When you're done reading the latest Read With Jenna pick, here's what to know.
Best Book to Screen
Imperfect Women
This psychological thriller explores guilt, retribution, love and betrayal through three fascinating perspectives.

Nancy Hennessy is murdered and leaves behind a secret: The identity of a lover who nobody knew about. Not her husband. Not her friends. Not her daughter. The adaptation is soon to air on AppleTV+ starring Elisabeth Moss and Kerry Washington.
Best Drama
Where the Wildflowers Grow: A Novel
Be inspired by Terah Shelton Harris' newest story of survival and redemption.

"Where the Wildflowers Grow" opens with a harrowing scene: A prison bus crashes over a ravine leaving only one survivor. Leigh survives and runs away to a flower farm in rural Alabama, starting a tale of hope and redemption.
Best Feel-Good Read
That's What Friends Are For
Inspired by The Golden Girls, this hilarious story celebrates love, aging, and finding your people.

Fans of "The Golden Girls" will love this book inspired by the show about friendship and aging in style. Theodore Copeland's estranged sister and her granddaughter show up at his oasis in Palm Springs, bringing secrets and disruption to his life with the "Golden Gays" (his nickname for his friend group).
Best Binge Read
Python's Kiss: Stories
Explore Louise Erdrich’s writing with this magnificent collection of her stories.

Louise Erdrich, whose last book was chosen for Read With Jenna's book club, has a new collection of short stories written from the past two decades of her storied career. According to the description from the publisher, the story collection features characters like "a tribal newsletter editor whose son tells her a story that nothing in her experience can encompass" and a girl who decides to spend her life with a stone.

title: Trump says he wants Iran's leadership structure gone and has preferences for a 'good leader'
summary: In a phone call with NBC News, the president also downplayed the possibility of a ground invasion in Iran.
story: WASHINGTON — President Donald Trump indicated Thursday that he wants to see Iran's leadership structure fully removed and that he has some names in mind for a "good leader."
"We want to go in and clean out everything," Trump told NBC News in a phone call. "We don’t want someone who would rebuild over a 10-year period.
“We want them to have a good leader. We have some people who I think would do a good job,” he added, declining to name anyone.
Trump also said he is taking steps to make sure the people on his list make it through the war alive.
"We are watching them, yeah," he said.

Trump's comments expand on remarks he made in an interview with NBC News on Saturday. Asked who will lead Iran next, Trump replied, “I don’t know, but at some point they’ll be calling me to ask who I’d like,” adding that he was “only being a little sarcastic when I say that.”
Trump also responded Thursday to Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi’s telling “NBC Nightly News” anchor Tom Llamas that his country is ready for a ground invasion by American and Israeli forces.
Trump called it a "wasted comment" and suggested an invasion is not something he is thinking about at this time.
“It’s a waste of time. They’ve lost everything. They’ve lost their navy. They’ve lost everything they can lose,” he said, adding that the pace and intensity of strikes will continue.
Iran faces a power vacuum after Khamenei died last weekend, with rumors swirling that his son Mojtaba Khamenei could be chosen as the new supreme leader.
The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, the dominant military, political and economic force in the country, is also poised to expand its power if it can survive the current conflict, analysts say.
Add NBC News to Google
Add NBC News to Google

On the domestic front, Trump reiterated his frustration that Congress has not passed the SAVE America Act, legislation that would impose new election requirements to register to vote, like proof of citizenship. The legislation is stalled in the Senate.
He said that he is “not happy it's not moving” and that he has “expressed that to everyone.”
He then went further than he has gone before by saying he would support a government shutdown if the bill does not make it to his desk.
“I would close government over it," Trump said. "To me, that’s a core belief.”

On Texas' Republican Senate primary, Trump did not say whom he would endorse in the race between Sen. John Cornyn and state Attorney General Ken Paxton, though he did note Paxton's support for the SAVE America Act.
At the same time, he said, “Cornyn is a very underrated person. He was supposed to lose by 10 points, and he won. He’s a good man.”
None of the three major candidates in the race was able to win a majority of the primary vote, prompting a May runoff between Cornyn and Paxton.

title: Oil hits $90 per barrel, stocks continue to drop as escalating Iran war shocks markets
summary: Oil prices continued soaring Friday, putting them on track to record their biggest jump since early 2020 as the escalating Iran war threatens global energy supplies.U.S.
story: Oil prices continued soaring Friday, putting them on track to record their biggest jump since early 2020 as the escalating Iran war threatens global energy supplies.
U.S. crude oil spiked more than 9% in early trading to more than $91 per barrel, its highest price since October 2023.
Brent, the international oil benchmark, broke $90 per barrel as it jumped more than 6% to its highest level since April 2024.

The move in prices came amid growing fears that the Iran war could lead to long-term energy supply issues. A report from The Wall Street Journal said that Kuwait had "begun cutting production at some oil fields after running out of room to store its bottled-up crude." NBC News was not immediately able to verify the report, but industry analysts have been warning of the possibility in recent days.

title: What we know about U.S. service members killed in the Iran war
summary: The U.S. government has said six U.S. service members have been killed in the military operation that started early Saturday with U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran.
story: The U.S. government has said six U.S. service members have been killed in the military operation that started early Saturday with U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran. Iran responded by launching missiles and drones in nearby countries.
At least five of the six U.S. service members, all of them Army reservists, died when a drone struck a port in Kuwait on Sunday, military officials said.
They were identified as Capt. Cody A. Khork, 35; Sgt. 1st Class Noah L. Tietjens, 42; Sgt. 1st Class Nicole M. Amor, 39; Spc. Declan J. Coady, 20; and Maj. Jeffrey O'Brien, 45.
A sixth soldier, Chief Warrant Officer Robert Marzan, is believed to have died at the scene of the attack, the Defense Department said.
Capt. Cody A. Khork
Khork, 35, was from Winter Haven, Florida. He enlisted in the National Guard in 2009 and was commissioned as a military police officer in the Army Reserve in 2014, the military said.
Khork's friend Abbas Jaffer posted photos of them together on Instagram.
"To my best friend, best man, and forever brother. I’m eternally grateful to have shared countless adventures, stories, and shenanigans with over the past 16 years," Jaffer said.
"You’re the kindest and best person I’ve ever known. There’s not enough words to describe how I feel," he added. "RIP my brother. Until we meet again."
Sgt. 1st Class Noah L. Tietjens
Tietjens, 42, was from Bellevue, Nebraska. Tietjens joined the Army Reserve as a wheeled vehicle mechanic in 2006.
Tietjens was a black belt in martial arts and was very dedicated to the craft, Julius Melegrito, one of the instructors at Martial Arts International in Bellevue, said in an interview.
Melegrito said that whenever Tietjens wasn't out on deployment, he was supporting his family's journey in martial arts and hanging out around the dojo.
Tietjens joined in 2008, and the family are all black belts now.
Melegrito described Tietjens as confident, respectful and easy to get along with. He said that when his wife met Tietjens, she said: "This is the kind of guy who always makes you feel welcome."
Tietjens would man the door at Martial Arts International events because of his "strict but friendly" nature, Melegrito said, calling Tietjens "the kind of person that gives."
"He died giving for his country," he said. Tietjens was "very well respected by others. He has that confidence, but he has that friendliness, as he deals with all different kinds of individuals."
Sgt. 1st Class Nicole M. Amor
Amor, 39, was from White Bear Lake, Minnesota. Amor joined the National Guard in 2005 and transferred to the Army Reserve in 2006, Army Reserve Command said. She deployed to Kuwait and Iraq in 2019.
Sen. Amy Klobuchar, D-Minn., said on X that Amor "made the ultimate sacrifice serving our country."
"Our hearts are with her family, loved ones, and all those in our armed forces," Klobuchar said.
Spc. Declan J. Coady
Coady, 20, was from West Des Moines, Iowa. Coady enlisted in the Army Reserve in 2023, the command said.
Coady was a student at Drake University in Des Moines. The school said on Facebook that it was "heartbroken" to learn of his death.
He was "a well-loved and highly dedicated Drake University student studying information systems, cybersecurity, and computer science," according to the post.

"He has bravely served in the U.S. Army Reserves since 2023 as an Information Technologies Specialist and had an incredibly bright future ahead of him," Coady's university said. "In moments like these, we must all come together to show much-needed love and compassion for the Coady family through this incredibly difficult time."
His sister Keira Coady described him Wednesday in a family statement as smart, kind, an avid gamer and a beloved member of ROTC at Drake University.
Coady died on his way to the hospital after he was injured in the airstrike in Kuwait, the statement said.

“On Sunday, March 1, after trying to check in, we heard nothing. Trying to be positive we all assumed he was just in a situation where he couldn’t message back, but we all knew something was wrong,” the statement said. “At 8 p.m. as we all were getting ready to go to bed, the doorbell rang, and the rest of that night will forever be one of the worst nights of our lives. While it’s all blurry, we all knew what the doorbell meant.”
Keira remembered her brother as reserved but passionate.
“He was supposed to be 21 on May 5. He was just a baby, and will forever be mine and Aidan’s baby brother, Rowan’s older brother, and our parents’ son,” she wrote.
“He was truly a rock in all of our lives and was just the most amazing brother and son my family could have asked for,” she added.

John Crandall, Coady’s uncle, remembered his nephew as an “incredibly gifted young man.” He was a former Eagle Scout who followed his older brother’s footsteps in joining the Army, with dreams of becoming an officer, Crandall told NBC’s “TODAY” show.
“There was just something beautiful about him, something amazing, something very intelligent about him,” he said. “He put himself in a position where many people will never go.”
Maj. Jeffrey O'Brien
O'Brien, 45, was from Indianola, Iowa, the Pentagon said.
Additional information was not immediately available.
Chief Warrant Officer 3 Robert Marzan

Marzan, 54, was from Sacramento, California. He is believed to have died at the scene of the drone attack, but medical officials are still awaiting positive identification from the medical examiner, the Pentagon said in a statement.
Additional information about a possible cause of death was not immediately available.
All six service members were assigned to the 103rd Sustainment Command based in Des Moines, military officials said.
In addition to those who died, 18 other U.S. service members have suffered serious injuries, a U.S. official told NBC News on Monday.

"We honor our fallen Heroes, who served fearlessly and selflessly in defense of our nation. Their sacrifice, and the sacrifices of their families, will never be forgotten,” Lt. Gen. Robert Harter, chief of Army Reserve and commanding general of U.S. Army Reserve Command, said in a statement.
Add NBC News to Google
Add NBC News to Google
The U.S. and Israel launched attacks in Iran around 1:15 a.m. ET Saturday, using “precision munitions launched from air, land and sea” and one-way drones, Central Command has said.
Iran’s Red Crescent said nearly 950 people have been killed in Israeli and American strikes. Iran’s supreme leader, Ali Khamenei, was also killed.

Iran responded to the U.S. and Israeli strikes by launching missiles and drones at targets in surrounding nations. Eleven deaths were reported in Israel. Deaths were also reported in the United Arab Emirates, Kuwait, Bahrain, Oman and Iraq. In Lebanon, 123 have been killed after Hezbollah and Israel exchanged fire.

Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said Tuesday that it launched 230 attack drones on a U.S. base in Erbil, Iraq, and the U.S. Ali Al Salem and Arifjan bases in Kuwait, the semiofficial Iranian news agency Tasnim reported.
The military action could last weeks, President Donald Trump said Monday. “We projected four to five weeks, but we have capability to go far longer than that,” he said.
Trump and other administration officials have said the U.S. attacked Iran because of an imminent threat and after nuclear talks failed to reach an agreement.

Some members of Congress have questioned that assessment and Trump’s power to attack Iran without seeking approval from Congress. Democrats and some Republicans plan to force a war powers vote that could restrict Trump’s ability to carry out further attacks in Iran.

title: Who is set to be in charge in Iran now that Khamenei is dead? A powerful hard-line military corps
summary: The U.S.-Israeli attack that ended Ayatollah Ali Khamenei’s iron-fisted rule of Iran also created a power vacuum in the Islamic Republic for the first time in decades, with a group of clerics convening to cast their votes on who would succeed him.
story: The U.S.-Israeli attack that ended Ayatollah Ali Khamenei’s iron-fisted rule of Iran also created a power vacuum in the Islamic Republic for the first time in decades, with a group of clerics convening to cast their votes on who would succeed him.

While front-runners to succeed Khamenei have emerged, including the assassinated supreme leader's second son, Mojtaba Khamenei, the real power is now likely to be in the hands of the heavily armed force that propped up Khamenei for decades: the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps.

“Even if they replace the supreme leader, what is left of the regime is the IRGC. And the IRGC is going to be the last vestige remaining of the regime until the regime is overhauled, either within itself or by external forces,” said Afshon Ostovar, an Iran expert at the Naval Postgraduate School in California and the author of “Vanguard of the Imam,” a history of the Revolutionary Guards.

“Once the smoke clears, if there’s not a complete regime change, then the people who will be in charge of Iran will be associated or the actual command of the IRGC,” he said, noting that his views are not the official position of the Department of Defense.

title: Dispatch from Tehran: Nowhere feels safe from attack
summary: TEHRAN, Iran — Nowhere in Tehran feels safe anymore.
story: TEHRAN, Iran — Nowhere in Tehran feels safe anymore.
The city is covered in smoke. The days feel ordinary, but the nights are terrifying. No one knows what might be targeted next.
Military bases and police stations are being struck, but police stations are often close to residential neighborhoods. One I passed today looked like something out of doomsday.
A friend of mine was near the police station in Niloofar Square when it was attacked.
The building was reduced to powder, and the residential buildings surrounding it were damaged, as well.
People are scared.

title: China emerges as big winner from Supreme Court's tariff ruling
summary: HONG KONG — Global trade has been upended again after the Supreme Court struck down President Donald Trump’s “reciprocal” tariffs, with U.S. trading partners and businesses around the world grasping to understand the system that replaces them
story: HONG KONG — Global trade has been upended again after the Supreme Court struck down President Donald Trump’s “reciprocal” tariffs, with U.S. trading partners and businesses around the world grasping to understand the system that replaces them.

A new flat global tariff of 10% paid by U.S. importers took effect Tuesday, lower than the 15% that Trump said he would implement days before. Under Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974, the 10% tariff can remain in place for 150 days without congressional approval.

China is the “biggest winner” from the Supreme Court ruling, with an effective U.S. tariff rate now much closer to that of other countries, said Alicia García-Herrero, chief economist for Asia-Pacific at French investment bank Natixis.
Among other benefits, China’s lower tariff rate reduces the incentive for companies to shift production to other countries in Asia, at least temporarily.

But the whiplash has created overwhelming uncertainty for key U.S. allies and some of Washington’s biggest trading partners, many of whom had already announced or were nearing trade deals with the U.S., some having offered major concessions with the aim of securing favorable rates under Trump’s now-defunct tariff regime.
In Asia, Trump administration officials had rushed to conclude deals in the weeks before the court ruling, with Indonesia agreeing to a 19% tariff rate only a day earlier.
The tariff ruling also comes just weeks before Trump’s upcoming trip to China, where he hopes to maintain a delicate trade truce with the world’s second-biggest economy.

Those who stand to lose the most include Japan and Taiwan, both of which had previously pledged hundreds of billions in U.S. investment in exchange for a tariff rate of 15%. Singapore and Australia also stand to lose since they already had a relatively favorable tariff rate of 10%.
“If you’re Taiwan, you’re like, why on earth did I commit to $250 billion if I’m getting the same tariffs?” said García-Herrero, who is based in Hong Kong.
The Trump administration has said that it will stand by existing deals and that it expects U.S. trading partners to do so as well.

“The good news is that almost all countries and corporations want to keep the deal that they already made,” Trump said Tuesday in his State of the Union address, “knowing that the legal power that I as president have to make a new deal could be far worse for them.”
Add NBC News to Google
Add NBC News to Google
A day earlier, Trump said in a social media post that any countries that tried to “play games” with trade deals after the court ruling would be hit with much higher tariffs.
Major trading partners in Asia, highly dependent on U.S. exports, are generally sticking within the terms of their existing deals or taking a wait-and-see approach.

India, which had planned to sign an agreement in March, delayed a scheduled trade delegation visit to Washington last week, saying talks would resume once there was more clarity. Its trade minister then had a surprise lunch meeting in New Delhi on Thursday with Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick.

While Trump officials say they expect the deals to be maintained, “point one of every deal is the reciprocal tariff rate,” said Deborah Elms, head of trade policy at the Hinrich Foundation in Singapore.
The primary question for many governments, she said, is whether their goods are now subject to their previous rates or the new 10% rate, which the White House has not clarified.
“That right there is causing a fair amount of anxiety over what is the actual rate, and then what happens to the rest of the agreement,” Elms said.
U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said last week that for some countries, which he did not name, tariff rates would rise to 15% or even higher while remaining compatible with existing deals.

He says that there are no plans to raise tariffs on Chinese goods ahead of Trump’s trip to China, which the White House says will begin March 31, and that he does not expect the Supreme Court ruling to affect Trump’s meeting with Chinese President Xi Jinping.

China, which responded to Trump’s first reciprocal tariffs last year with mounting tariffs of its own, said after the ruling that it would “comprehensively assess” any future U.S. tariff changes and adjust its countermeasures as appropriate.
“The current situation has placed China in a relatively comfortable position,” Chinese international affairs commentator Qiu Zhenhai said in a social media post.

“This is because Trump’s proposed high tariffs — whether directed at the world or specifically at China — are currently being constrained by supreme legal authorities internally and, frankly speaking, have triggered significant backlash within the United States.”
But China still has a lot to worry about, such as Trump’s ability to impose export controls on strategically vital products such as semiconductor chips.

According to Greer, one of the main ways the Trump administration plans to replace the reciprocal tariffs is through investigations, permitted under Section 301 of the 1974 Trade Act, of countries accused of treating the U.S. or its companies unfairly.

Elms said those investigations, along with national security probes focused on strategic industries under the same law, could provide the White House with a more solid basis to impose fresh tariffs, as well as other punitive measures that could end up being far worse for some countries than the tariffs previously struck down.

Though Greer has indicated a desire to move swiftly on Section 301 investigations, it would be difficult to exclude China from any list of countries facing investigations, which would risk antagonizing Beijing before Trump’s trip, Elms said.
Greer said his office was also continuing an existing Section 301 investigation into China that stems from Trump’s first-term trade deal with Beijing and could be used to justify steep new tariffs.
China said last week that it had “earnestly fulfilled its obligations” under that deal.
The Supreme Court ruling also did not affect sector-specific U.S. tariffs on automobiles and steel, which are crucial industries for China, as well as South Korea and Japan.

On Tuesday, the president of Hyundai Motor, one of South Korea’s biggest carmakers, urged lawmakers to swiftly approve the $350 billion in pledged U.S. investment, citing the risk of higher sectoral tariffs.
Elms said that many U.S. trade partners will “have domestic politics of their own” to consider.

She added: “It’s really getting very challenging to manage a government-to-government relationship with the U.S. and we will see some of that irritation, I think, spill over as the tariff rates keep changing and these deals appear to be more or less desirable.”

title: Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, religious cleric who ruled Iran for decades, killed in strikes
summary: Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who reigned as Iran’s supreme leader for the past 36 years, was killed in a sweeping U.S. and Israeli attack on the country Saturday.
story: Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who reigned as Iran’s supreme leader for the past 36 years, was killed in a sweeping U.S. and Israeli attack on the country Saturday. He was 86.
President Donald Trump announced his death on social media and state TV later confirmed it.

To Khamenei’s critics, he was a despot who consolidated power over nearly four decades, bolstered the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corp to become the most powerful military and economic force in the country, and clung to power in his final days with a brutal crackdown on anti-government protesters that killed thousands.
To his supporters, he will be revered as a martyr who stood up to the aggression of the U.S. and Israel, which he said should be wiped off the map.

His death leaves a nation that he ruled with an iron grip for decades unmoored, possibly heading to a period of great turmoil.  Two opposing factions, the protesters pushing for greater freedom and the heavily armed security forces confronting them, are now vying for power while American and Israeli bombs continue to fall.
“Khamenei, one of the most evil people in History, is dead,” Trump posted Saturday on Truth Social.

title: Iran's foreign minister denies seeking war with its neighbors
summary: Since the U.S. and Israel launched attacks across Iran last weekend, the Islamic Republic has retaliated by firing missiles and drones across the region, striking Bahrain, Qatar, the United Arab Emirates and other countries.
story: Since the U.S. and Israel launched attacks across Iran last weekend, the Islamic Republic has retaliated by firing missiles and drones across the region, striking Bahrain, Qatar, the United Arab Emirates and other countries.
But Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said Thursday in an interview with "NBC Nightly News" anchor Tom Llamas that Iran had not made a decision to start a war with its neighbors.

"We have not attacked our neighbors. We have not attacked Muslim countries," Araghchi said. "We have attacked American targets and American bases, American installations, which are unfortunately located in the soils of our neighbors."
Araghchi said he has spoken with the foreign ministers of these countries to explain that they are not the target.
Add NBC News to Google
Add NBC News to Google

Llamas noted that some of the Iranian attacks have hit civilian targets, including residential areas in Bahrain, hotels in Dubai and the international airport in Kuwait, but Araghchi dismissed any such attacks as "collateral damage."
The instability in the region has led to a spike in oil prices and a drop in the U.S. stock market.
For years, Iran has threatened to turn the Strait of Hormuz, a key waterway through which 20% of the world's oil passes, into a free-fire zone if the country is attacked.

It would not be the first time that marine traffic has been disrupted in the Persian Gulf: During the Iran-Iraq war in the 1980s, dozens of merchant vessels were attacked by both parties in what broadly became known as the "tanker war."
On Monday, Iranian Revolutionary Guard Brig. Gen. Ebrahim Jabbari told state TV that the strait is closed and that any ships passing through would be set on fire.
Asked by Llamas about this threat, Araghchi said there had been no threat and the strait is open.

"They have not closed it. It is the ships and tankers who don’t try to pass through the, to cross the strait, because they are, you know, concerned about being hit by either side," he said. "So we have no intention to close it right now, but as the war continues, we will consider every scenario."

The naval war has already spread outside the region. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said Wednesday that a U.S. submarine had sunk an Iranian military ship with a torpedo in the Indian Ocean, a move that Araghchi, in a post on X, said would set a precedent the U.S. would "bitterly regret."

In the interview with Llamas, Araghchi said that the ship was unarmed and on a training exercise and that the attack, which killed 87 sailors, was a "war crime," though he did not provide any evidence to support the claim.
"You know, when an unarmed ship is attacked with no reason, and group of big number of sailors are killed without taking part in any battle, that would set a precedent," he said.
The spread of the war across the region has also raised the question of whether Iran's key allies, Russia and China, may join the fray in some form.
"They are supporting us politically, and otherwise," Araghchi said, but would not get into details of whether that includes military support.
